# Preop Esophageal Ca {#preop_esophageal}

See [Esophageal Cancer Lecture](https://gisurgonc.github.io/talks/eso_ca_rx_talk.htm)

## Operative Morality with Liver Disease

30-day postoperative mortality: Approximately 1–2% per MELD point below a score of 20, translating to a roughly ~10% estimated 30-day mortality for nontransplant intraabdominal surgery based on the linear model described by Northup et al. (1 percentage point increase per MELD point). 
[1-2]

90-day postoperative mortality: In a study of 490 cirrhotic patients undergoing surgery under general anesthesia, the 90-day mortality for MELD scores 10–14 was 8.9%, compared to 3.5% for MELD 6–9. A MELD score ≥10 was an independent risk factor for 90-day mortality on multivariable analysis. 
[3]

Hepatectomy: For patients undergoing hepatic resection, 30-day mortality was 1.8% for MELD 0–9 and 6.9% for MELD 10–19, with a 16% increase in odds of mortality per each additional MELD point. 
[4]

Emergency general surgery: Compared to MELD 6–9.9, patients with MELD 10–19.9 had 2-fold higher unadjusted odds of 90-day mortality (OR 1.99; 95% CI 1.25–3.16), though after adjustment for confounders this was attenuated (adjusted OR 1.45; 95% CI 0.89–2.38). 
[5]

Key context: Patients with a MELD score up to 12 are generally considered at very low risk of mortality at 3 months, even with major surgical procedures. 
[6] A MELD score >14 is a better threshold than Child-Pugh class C for predicting high surgical risk. 
[1] The Mayo Clinic has developed an [online surgical risk calculator](www.mayoclinic.org/medical-professionals/model-end-stage-liver-disease/post-operative-mortality-risk-patients-cirrhosis) that incorporates MELD score, age, and ASA class for more individualized risk estimation. 
[1]

The type of surgery significantly modifies risk — emergency surgery, open abdominal procedures, and cardiovascular surgery carry substantially higher mortality than elective or lower-risk procedures at the same MELD score. 
[3][7]


1.
Treatment of Patients with Cirrhosis.
The New England Journal of Medicine. 2016. Ge PS, Runyon BA.Review
2.
Model for End-Stage Liver Disease (MELD) Predicts Nontransplant Surgical Mortality in Patients With Cirrhosis.
Annals of Surgery. 2005. Northup PG, Wanamaker RC, Lee VD, Adams RB, Berg CL.
3.
Mortality After Surgery in Patients With Liver Cirrhosis: Comparison of Child-Turcotte-Pugh, MELD and MELDNa Score.
European Journal of Gastroenterology & Hepatology. 2011. Cho HC, Jung HY, Sinn DH, et al.
4.
Mortality in Hepatectomy: Model for End-Stage Liver Disease as a Predictor of Death Using the National Surgical Quality Improvement Program Database.
Surgery. 2016. Ross SW, Seshadri R, Walters AL, et al.
5.
Association of Model for End-Stage Liver Disease Score With Mortality in Emergency General Surgery Patients.
JAMA Surgery. 2016. Havens JM, Columbus AB, Olufajo OA, et al.Observational
6.
Liver Cirrhosis.
Lancet. 2021. Ginès P, Krag A, Abraldes JG, et al.Review
7.
Acute-on-Chronic Liver Failure Clinical Guidelines.
The American Journal of Gastroenterology. 2022. Bajaj JS, O'Leary JG, Lai JC, et al.Guideline

Esophagectomy in a cirrhotic patient with a MELD score of 10 carries substantially elevated operative mortality compared to noncirrhotic patients, and notably, a MELD score >9 has been identified as a critical threshold for worse outcomes after esophagectomy specifically.

Esophagectomy-Specific Data

In a single-center matched-cohort study [@valmasoni763] (73 cirrhotic patients from 3,445 esophageal cancer patients), cirrhotic patients with a MELD score >9 had a significantly worse postoperative course and poorer 5-year survival (p = 0.004) compared to those with MELD ≤9, who had outcomes similar to noncirrhotic patients. overall 90-day mortality rate was 15.1 % in the cirrhotic group (MELD >9) and 8.9 % in the No-CIRR group MELD ≤9(p = 0.309). The 5-year survival rates were comparable in the two groups (p = 0.397).


A smaller single-center series (14 cirrhotic patients, most with MELD ≤9) reported strikingly high 30-day mortality of 57.1% and 90-day mortality of 64.3% among cirrhotic patients undergoing esophagectomy, compared to 7.7% and 9.6% in noncirrhotic patients. Notably, the MELD and Child scores failed to predict perioperative mortality in this cohort; instead, bilirubin (auROC 94.4%) and INR (auROC 90.0%) were the strongest individual predictors. 
[@grasse0265093]

A meta-analysis of 12 observational studies (1,938 patients) confirmed that cirrhotic patients undergoing esophagectomy have 3-fold higher 30-day mortality (OR 3.04; 95% CI 1.71–5.39), along with significantly higher rates of pulmonary complications (OR 2.60), anastomotic leak (OR 2.81), and ascites (OR 37.77). Child-Pugh A patients had significantly lower 30-day mortality than Child-Pugh B patients (OR 0.14; 95% CI 0.04–0.54). [@schizas865]


Even among Child-Pugh A cirrhotic patients undergoing esophagectomy, a propensity-matched study showed significantly higher rates of postoperative pneumonia (22% vs. 9%), pleural effusion (38% vs. 20%), and chylothorax (10% vs. 1%) compared to noncirrhotic patients. 
[5]

Risk Stratification Considerations

The 2025 ACG Clinical Guideline on Perioperative Risk Assessment in Cirrhosis emphasizes that MELD score alone is inadequate for surgical risk prediction because it does not account for surgery type or nonhepatic comorbidities. The VOCAL-Penn Score is recommended as the preferred cirrhosis-specific surgical risk calculator, as it incorporates surgery type and has been shown to outperform the Mayo Surgical Risk Score in discrimination and calibration. 
[6] For a complex procedure like esophagectomy, this distinction is particularly important.

Bottom line: A MELD of 10 places this patient above the critical threshold of 9 identified for esophagectomy, where outcomes diverge significantly from noncirrhotic patients. While cirrhosis is not an absolute contraindication to esophagectomy, the decision warrants careful multidisciplinary evaluation incorporating bilirubin, INR, presence of ascites, and ideally the VOCAL-Penn Score rather than relying on MELD alone. 
[1-2][6]




2.
Risk Stratification of Cirrhotic Patients Undergoing Esophagectomy for Esophageal Cancer: A Single-Centre Experience.
PloS One. 2022. Grass JK, Küsters N, Kemper M, et al.


5.
Impact of Child-Pugh Class a Liver Cirrhosis on Perioperative Outcomes of Patients With Oesophageal Cancer: A Propensity Score-Matched Analysis.
European Journal of Cardio-Thoracic Surgery : Official Journal of the European Association for Cardio-Thoracic Surgery. 2020. Cheng C, Wen YW, Tsai CY, Chao YK.
6.
ACG Clinical Guideline: Perioperative Risk Assessment and Management in Patients With Cirrhosis.
The American Journal of Gastroenterology. 2025. Mahmud N, Fricker ZP, McElroy LM, et al.RecentGuideline
